title: Cost-volume-profit analysis
summary: 9706 — break-even, margin of safety, target profit, CVP charts, and assumptions for Paper 2.
learningObjectives: Calculate break-even point in units and revenue.
Determine margin of safety in units and percentage.
Calculate sales volume for a target profit.
Interpret CVP charts and state assumptions.

simpleExplanation.title: How much must we sell?
simpleExplanation.summary: CVP analysis uses contribution to find break-even — where total contribution equals fixed costs — and how far sales can fall before the business makes a loss.
simpleExplanation.analogy: A food truck must pay $200 daily site fee (fixed) plus $3 ingredients per meal (variable). Each $10 meal contributes $7 toward the fee. Break-even is about 29 meals — after that, every sale is profit until the day ends.
steps: Contribution per unit = SP − VC per unit.
BEP (units) = fixed costs ÷ contribution per unit.
Margin of safety = budgeted sales − BEP.
Target profit: (FC + target profit) ÷ contribution per unit.

flashcards.back: Fixed costs ÷ (Contribution per unit ÷ Selling price), i.e. FC ÷ C/S ratio.
Budgeted or actual sales units − Break-even units.
(Fixed costs + Target profit) ÷ Contribution per unit.

flashcards.front: What does a CVP chart show?
flashcards.back: FC line (horizontal), TC line (sloping), TR line (from origin) — intersection = break-even.
9706 Paper 2 expects full accounting working, charts, and clear labels; same core formulas as business studies.
Selling price per unit does not change with volume.
Variable cost per unit stays constant as output changes.
Shows risk (margin of safety), required sales for profit targets, and effect of cost/price changes.
Need constant sales mix or weighted average contribution per unit.

faq.q: Is CVP the same as break-even analysis in 9609?
faq.a: The core maths is identical. 9706 Paper 2 may require more formal accounting presentation, journal awareness, and chart drawing; 9609 adds more evaluation of business context.
When assumptions break down: discounts change SP, step costs increase FC, product mix shifts, or output is far outside the relevant range.

sections.content: **Cost-volume-profit (CVP) analysis** (2.2.4) is a core **9706 Paper 2** topic. Examiners expect **neat calculations**, **labelled CVP charts**, and **written assumptions**. Use the interactive chart alongside this lesson — the same relationships apply as in **9609 break-even (5.4.4)**.

Contribution per unit = Selling price − Variable cost per unit

Break-even (units) = $\frac{\text{Fixed costs}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$

Break-even (revenue) = $\frac{\text{Fixed costs}}{\text{C/S ratio}}$ where C/S = contribution ÷ selling price
At break-even, **total contribution = fixed costs** and profit is zero. All fixed costs must be covered by contribution from units sold before profit is earned.
Margin of safety and target profit

Margin of safety (units) = Budgeted sales − Break-even sales

Margin of safety (%) = $\frac{\text{MoS units}}{\text{Budgeted sales}} \times 100$

Sales for target profit = $\frac{\text{FC} + \text{Target profit}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$

sections.question:
The following information relates to a single product:

Selling price $25 per unit
Variable cost $10 per unit
Fixed costs $18 000 per month
Budgeted sales 1 500 units

Calculate (a) break-even in units, (b) margin of safety in units and %, (c) units required for $12 000 profit.
Using the same data, calculate break-even **revenue**.

sections.content:
Draw **fixed costs** horizontal, **total cost** from FC upward with slope = variable cost per unit, **total revenue** from origin with slope = selling price. Mark break-even and shade profit/loss regions.

**Assumptions:** single product or constant mix; constant SP and VC per unit; fixed costs unchanged; production = sales (no inventory change) unless stated.
Paper 2 marking often splits marks between **calculation**, **chart**, and **comment**. Always state formulas before substituting numbers and label axes on any graph.
